lines.content: This is a project template for a greenfield Java project. It's named after the Java mascot _Duke_. Given below are instructions on how to use it.
Prerequisites: JDK 17, update Intellij to the most recent version.
1. Open Intellij (if you are not in the welcome screen, click `File` > `Close Project` to close the existing project first)
1. Open the project into Intellij as follows:
1. Select the project directory, and click `OK`.
1. If there are any further prompts, accept the defaults.
1. Configure the project to use **JDK 17** (not other versions) as explained in [here](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/idea/sdk.html#set-up-jdk).
In the same dialog, set the **Project language level** field to the `SDK default` option.

1. After that, locate the `src/main/java/Greet.java` file, right-click it, and choose `Run Greet.main()` (if the code editor is showing compile errors, try restarting the IDE). If the setup is correct, you should see something like the below as the output:

**Warning:** Keep the `src\main\java` folder as the root folder for Java files (i.e., don't rename those folders or move Java files to another folder outside of this folder path), as this is the default location some tools (e.g., Gradle) expect to find Java files.

Ragebait is a command-line interface (CLI) application that helps users manage tasks, deadlines, and contacts efficiently. It allows users to:
- Add, view, find, mark, unmark, and delete tasks
- Maintain a contact list with names, phone numbers, and emails
- Add, view, delete, and find contacts
**Java 17 or above** must be installed on your system.
**Mac users:** Ensure your JDK version matches the app’s requirements.
1. Download the latest ragebait.jar from the releases page
2. Choose a folder to serve as your home folder for Ragebait.
3. Copy the ragebait.jar file into this folder.
4. Launch the app by running the jar file: ```java -jar ragebait.jar```
Here is the overview of all commands in the CLI application.
| Add ToDo Task|`task todo <description>`|`task todo Buy milk`|
| Add Deadline Task | `task deadline <description> /by <d/M/YYYY HHMM>` | `task deadline Submit report /by 25/2/2026 2359` |
| Add Event Task| `task event <description> /from <d/M/YYYY HHMM> /to <d/M/YYYY HHMM>` | `task event Team meeting /from 25/2/2026 1400 /to 25/2/2026 1500` |
| Add Contact | `contact add /name <name> /phone <phone> /email <email>` | `contact add /name Jack /phone 91234567 /email jack@example.com` |
Goodbye. Go touch some grass. Or don’t. Not my problem anymore.
* Extraneous parameters for this command will be ignored. e.g `bye 123` will still terminate the app.
Adds a new ToDo task to your task list.
Congrats. Another responsibility you’ll probably ignore:
That makes 1 task. Collecting them like regrets.
* The ToDo description must not be empty.
* The task category must be present.
Adds a new Deadline task to your task list.
Congrats. Another responsibility you’ll probably ignore:
That makes 2 tasks. Collecting them like regrets.
* The task category must be present.
* The Deadline description and /by datetime must not be empty.
* The /by date accepts both single- and double-digit day/month values. Examples: `1/1/2021` and `01/01/2021` are both valid.
* The date and time provided must be valid calendar values.
* Time is in 24-hour format (`HHMM`) without a colon.
* `2400` is accepted and treated as midnight of the following day.
Adds a new Event task to your task list.
Congrats. Another responsibility you’ll probably ignore:
That makes 3 tasks. Collecting them like regrets.
* The task category must be present.
* The Event description and /from datetime and /to datetime must not be empty.
* The /from and /to date accepts both single- and double-digit day/month values. Examples: `1/1/2021` and `01/01/2021` are both valid.
* The date and time provided must be valid calendar values.
* Time is in 24-hour format (`HHMM`) without a colon.
* `2400` is accepted and treated as midnight of the following day.
* The /from date must be strictly before the /to date.
Shows a list of all tasks.
Brace yourself. Here’s your disaster lineup:
* The task category must be present.
* Extraneous parameters for this command will be ignored. e.g `task list` will still show the list of tasks.
Deletes an existing task from the task list.
You’re down to 4 task(s). Try not to mess those up too.
* The task category must be present.
* <number> must be a valid integer corresponding to an existing task in the list.
Marks an existing task from the task list as complete.
Wait… you actually completed something? Miracles happen:
* The task category must be present.
* <number> must be a valid integer corresponding to an existing task in the list.
* Ragebait will prompt the user if they attempt to mark a task that is already marked.
Unmarks an existing task from the task list as incomplete.
* The task category must be present.
* Ragebait will prompt the user if they attempt to unmark a task that is already marked.
Searches the task list for tasks that contain the given keyword in their <description>.
Search complete. These are the only things matching your vague input:
* The task category must be present.
* Ragebait will prompt the user and will not return any results if the task is not found.
Adds a new contact to your contacts list.
Wow, a new contact. Someone actually tolerates you:
You now have 1 contact(s). Networking era?
* The contact category must be present.
* The /name, /phone and /email tags must be present.
* The phone number must be strictly 8 digits.
* Email must be in correct syntax
- Must include exactly one `@` symbol separating the local part and the domain part.
1. Contain letters, digits, dots (`.`), and hyphens (`-`)
2. Include at least one dot (`.`) to separate the domain name and top-level domain (TLD)
3. End with a valid TLD (e.g., `.com`, `.org`, `.net`, 2–6 letters)
- No spaces are allowed in the email.
- Consecutive dots in the local or domain part are not allowed (optional for stricter validation).
Shows a list of all contacts.
Here’s the full list of people who haven’t blocked you yet:
* The contact category must be present.
* Extraneous parameters for this command will be ignored. e.g `task contact` will still show the list of contacts.
Deletes an existing contact from the contact list.
You’re left with 1 contact(s). Choose wisely next time.
* The contact category must be present.
* <number> must be a valid integer corresponding to an existing contact in the list.
Searches the contact list for contacts whose <name> contain the specified keyword.
Search done. These are your so-called contacts:
* The contact category must be present.
* Ragebait will prompt the user and will not return any results if the contact is not found.

* Represents an executable command in the application.
* All concrete command classes must implement this interface.

* If something goes wrong during execution, a RagebaitException
* should be thrown with a message that clearly explains what the
* @param ui The UI component used to generate user-facing messages.

* @return A message to be displayed to the user.
* @throws RagebaitException If execution fails due to invalid input or other user-induced chaos.
String execute(UI ui, Context context) throws RagebaitException;
* Represents the different types of commands supported

* a RagebaitException will be thrown to gently remind them
* that imagination is not a supported feature.

* Unmarks a previously completed task by its index.

* Represents an abstract base class for all contact-related commands.
* Any command that operates on contacts should extend this class.
* This ensures a clear separation between contact commands and
* other command categories such as task commands.
* Subclasses must implement the execute method and define
* the specific contact-related behavior. If execution fails,
* they are expected to throw a RagebaitException with a message
* that makes it painfully obvious what went wrong.
public abstract class ContactCommand implements Command {
* @param ui The UI component used to generate user-facing messages.
* @param context The execution context containing shared application state, including contact data and storage.
* @return A message to be displayed to the user.
* @throws RagebaitException If execution fails due to invalid input or contact-related errors.
public abstract String execute(UI ui, Context context) throws RagebaitException;
* Represents the execution context shared across commands.
* This class acts as a central holder for application state,
* bundling together task-related data and contact-related data.
* Instead of passing four separate parameters everywhere
* and making constructors look like a mess, we wrap them
* All fields are immutable references to ensure that commands
* cannot casually swap out core components and break everything.

* The list of contacts managed by the application.

* The minimum valid index for contacts.

* Removes the contact at the specified index from the contact list,
* updates storage, and returns a UI message confirming deletion.
* @param ui The UI component used to generate feedback messages.
* @param context The execution context containing contacts and storage.
* @throws RagebaitException If the index is out of bounds.
public String execute(UI ui, Context context) throws RagebaitException {
"I CAN'T DELETE! THIS CONTACT DOES NOT EXIST! PAY ATTENTION NEXT TIME."

* Represents the command to exit the Ragebait application.

* with a message that basically screams, "Fix your storage!"

* and returns a UI-generated goodbye message.

* Custom checked exception for the Ragebait application.
* Thrown whenever the user messes up input, or when the application
* encounters a situation it cannot handle. Each exception carries
* a message that can be displayed to the user in a rage-flavored tone.
* Constructs a RagebaitException with the specified error message.
* @param message The detail message explaining why Ragebait is mad at the user.
* Constructs a RagebaitException with a message and a root cause.
* @param message The detail message explaining the rage-worthy problem.
* @param cause   The underlying exception that triggered this rage.
* Responsible for converting raw user input into executable Command objects.
* - Extracting the command category (task/contact) and command type.
* - Validating that required arguments are present.
* This class does not validate deeper business rules like
* proper date formats or email correctness; that is delegated
* If the user messes up, the parser throws rage-level RagebaitExceptions
* with clear instructions for fixing the disaster.
* Parses a raw user input string into the corresponding Command object.
* @return The Command object representing the user's intention.
* @throws RagebaitException If the input is empty, unknown, or missing required arguments.
public static Command parse(String fullCommand) throws RagebaitException {

private static TaskCommand parseTaskCommand(CommandType commandType, String args) throws RagebaitException {
requireArgs(args, "Specify an existing task number! Check using command task list!");
requireArgs(args, "Specify an existing task number! Check using command task list!");
requireArgs(args, "Specify an existing task number! Check using command task list!");
